issue: Heading order is semantically incorrect. The heading level jumps unexpectedly (e.g., from h3 or h4 to h5) without incrementing by one, causing confusion in document structure navigation.
affectedUsers: Users relying on screen readers or other assistive technologies who navigate through content by heading levels, including users with visual impairments and cognitive disabilities.
fixRecommendation: Adjust the heading levels to follow a logical, sequential order, ensuring heading levels only increase by one at a time (e.g., h2 followed by h3, not h5). Review the hierarchy of headings and correct the levels accordingly.

issue: Multiple landmarks with the same role (e.g., 'navigation') exist on the page without unique accessible names, making it difficult for assistive technology users to distinguish between them.
affectedUsers: Users of screen readers or assistive technologies who rely on landmarks for faster navigation, primarily users with visual impairments.
fixRecommendation: Provide unique accessible names for each landmark by adding aria-label, aria-labelledby, or a title attribute to distinguish landmarks with the same role (e.g., aria-label="Primary navigation", aria-label="Secondary navigation").

issue: The page does not contain a level-one heading (<h1>), which is important to define the main topic or purpose of the page.
affectedUsers: Users of screen readers and assistive technologies who rely on the main heading to understand page context quickly, including users with visual impairments and cognitive disabilities.
fixRecommendation: Add a level-one heading (<h1>) that clearly describes the main topic or purpose of the page. Ensure it is present and visible at the top-level of the content.
